2
P H Y S I C A L L AY E R
Differential Signaling & Voltage Levels
2
■Two complementary wires. Data is the voltage difference
between CAN_H and CAN_L, not an absolute level.
■Recessive = logic 1. Both lines rest near 2.5 V, so the
differential voltage is ~0 V.
■Dominant = logic 0. CAN_H rises to ~3.5 V and CAN_L drops to
~1.5 V, giving ~2 V difference.
■ISO 11898-2 high-speed CAN. Defines these levels for rates
up to 1 Mbit/s.
■ISO 11898-3 low-speed CAN. Fault-tolerant variant runs up to
125 kbit/s for body and comfort electronics.
High-speed CAN (ISO 11898-2) nominal levels
State
CAN_H
CAN_L
Differential
Logic
Recessive
2.5 V 2.5 V ~0 V 1
Dominant
3.5 V 1.5 V ~2.0 V 0
Why differential? Interference couples almost equally
onto both wires, so it cancels in the difference. That
common-mode rejection is what lets CAN survive the
harsh electrical environment of a vehicle.
CAN_H and CAN_L over time
3.5 V
2.5 V
1.5 V
Recessive
Logic 1
Dominant
Logic 0
Recessive
Logic 1
Dominant
Logic 0
CAN_H
CAN_L
≈2 V
P H Y S I C A L L AY E R
Bit Rate vs. Maximum Bus Length
3
■Up to 1 Mbit/s. Classic CAN reaches full speed only on
a short bus — about 40 m at 1 Mbit/s.
■Longer bus, slower bits. As length grows past that
limit, the maximum bit rate must come down.
■500 kbit/s in practice. Most production networks
settle here to trade speed against bus length.
How many frames fit at 500 kbit/s?
•500 kbit/s carries 500,000 bits every second.
•One frame is roughly 125 bits (about 111 bits before stuffing).
500,000 ÷ 125 ≈ 2,000 frames per second
Maximum bit rate falls as the bus gets longer
1 Mbit/s @ 40 m
500 kbit/s — typical
1000
100
10
1
Bit Rate [kbps]
40 200 1 000 10 000
CAN Bus Length [m]
Illustrative log–log trend; limits depend on transceivers and twisted-pair cabling.

P H Y S I C A L L AY E R · W I R I N G
Termination, Transceivers & Bus Faults
4
■Why terminate. Each bus end carries a 120 Ω resistor that absorbs
signal reflections; without it bits ring and corrupt.
■≈60 Ω healthy bus. The two 120 Ω resistors sit in parallel, so a good idle
bus measures ≈60 Ω across CAN_H–CAN_L.
■The transceiver. Converts the controller's TX/RX logic into differential
bus levels and back, with fault and ESD protection.
■Length vs. speed. Propagation delay caps the rate — about 1 Mbit/s at
40 m, falling as the bus gets longer.
120Ω 120Ω
ECU ECU ECU
COMMON BUS FAULTS
Fault
Typical symptom
CAN_H
–CAN_L short
Bus stuck dominant, no traffic
Missing / open
terminator
Reflections, sporadic CRC errors
Broken wire or ground
Nodes drop off; bus
-off events
Field check: power down and measure across CAN_H–
CAN_L — ≈60 Ω means both terminators are present and
healthy.
Why a healthy bus reads ≈ 60 Ω
120Ω 120Ω
Ω≈ 60 Ω
120 Ω ∥ 120 Ω = 60 Ω
Power the bus down and measure across CAN_H–CAN_L; ≈60 Ω confirms
both terminators are present.
P H Y S I C A L L AY E R
Reading CAN on an Oscilloscope
6
What you probe
Recessive (logic 1)
Dominant (logic 0)
CAN_H vs. GND
Rests near 2.5 V
Driven up to about 3.5 V
CAN_L vs. GND
Rests near 2.5 V
Pulled down to about 1.5 V
Differential (H − L)
Near 0 V
— lines converge
About 2 V separation
Edges & ringing
Flat, quiet baseline
Clean fast edges if terminated
Bit width
Constant across the frame
Set by the configured bit rate
Probe CAN_H and CAN_L against ground and watch them split apart on every dominant bit. Rounded edges, overshoot, or ringing on the
trace usually point to missing or incorrect 120 Ω termination.
B U S L O G I C
Dominant, Recessive & Wired-AND
7
The bus behaves like a wired-AND: if any node drives a dominant bit, the whole bus is dominant. This single rule underpins
both arbitration and error signalling.
Two nodes driving the bus simultaneously
Node A = 1
Node B = 1
Bus = 1
Recessive
Node A = 1
Node B = 0
Bus = 0
Dominant
Node A = 0
Node B = 1
Bus = 0
Dominant
Node A = 0
Node B = 0
Bus = 0
Dominant
Key consequence: dominant bits (logic 0) always overwrite recessive bits (logic 1). A transmitter can therefore listen while it sends and
instantly detect when it has lost the bus or when an error must be flagged.

Glossary & Key Acronyms
8
A quick-reference card for the terms used throughout this deck.
Term
Meaning
SOF / EOF
Start / End of Frame delimiters
DLC
Data Length Code (payload size)
RTR / IDE
Remote request / Identifier
-extension bits
CRC
Cyclic Redundancy Check field
ACK
Acknowledgement slot
TEC / REC
Transmit / Receive Error Counters
Term
Meaning
CSMA/CR
Carrier
-sense access with collision resolution
DBC
CAN signal database (messages & signals)
ISO
-TP
ISO 15765
-2 transport for long data
UDS
Unified Diagnostic Services
SecOC
Secure Onboard Communication
ECU
Electronic Control Unit (a node)
